item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the pic is real good and i'll remember the use of the word 'pomace'. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Clever without trying to make things seem more impressive than the idea is by being obtuse or redacting too much information. Like other SCP's from this author, this one seems underappreciated,

I agree with PeppersGhost about using bolds for headings in the test logs. Not only does it make the document easier to read, it is the usual practice in preparing a scientific manuscript (underlining or bold and underlined are also used). I suppose that the Results section should be split into a brief Materials and Methods and a Results section. Not really critical though it would allow saying "Class D personnel were acquired in the usual manner." Which takes the scientific veracity up to 11 on a 10 point scale. Regardless, if I ever write an SCP, I am most definitely stealing this format.

Great clinical tone, as one would expect from a clinician (author is a CRNP I think).
+1

I just wish the author's account hadn't been deleted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is quite good. There are some really novel flourishes in this, like the way you connected this to AWCY? without the use of a signed note. I also appreciate the Conclusion/Discussion section of the test log; usually that kind of thing would be included in the description, but this placing makes sense and keeps your test log results from getting spoiled too early. I may have to steal that trick sometime.

The only suggestion I'd make it is to use bolding instead of italics in the test logs, since it gives the eye someplace to rest and creates a clearer hierarchy of information, but that's a small detail. Enthusiastic +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I assume the bisection is from impacting the hook during the ABE

Yes, this was an occurrence that I intentionally didn't make explicit, very glad you picked up on it too.

the figures ascend being stopped and their still being alive just seems to introduce new information which doesn't point anywhere (though I suppose it implies the pulped bodies were still alive).

The event points backwards, to SCP-3436-i, which is a really gruesome interpretation of prophecies regarding "the Rapture". Upon re-reading, the inscription becomes descriptive of the artist's ultimate endpoint for those who touch it.

So theoretically, there are several "corpses"/"pulps" stationary in the atmosphere that the Foundation has or has not yet incinerated.

item.title: Re: Licensing Team Post
item.content:encoded:
Permission is pending.

Redone image is from Wiki Commons, original and its details can be seen here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can't quite say I grasp what the last addendum is trying to imply, so although the imagery is striking it seems like a bit of an odd note to end on. I assume the bisection is from impacting the hook during the ABE, but the figures ascend being stopped and their still being alive just seems to introduce new information which doesn't point anywhere (though I suppose it implies the pulped bodies were still alive).

item.title: Licensing Team Post
item.content:encoded: Images have been provisionally removed due to uncertainty with their licensing status. Before the author readds them, they must clarify their licensing here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I appreciate your recognition for the scientific layout for the tests!

I get your critique of including the word “been” in “have been consented”, excluding it makes the phrase less passive. However, this is the way we (or at least some of us) in the medical field phrase a patient’s consent for a procedure, so for that reason and to make the flow a bit smoother, I’ll leave it as is. Technically, I agree with you though.

Thanks again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to say, this article turned out to be nothing like what the picture made it thought it would be. I don't quite know how to express what I think of it as a whole, but I can say that I enjoyed the test results. Not just because they were unexpected (I thought that the D9Rs would go flying, but what happened was so much worse than that!), but I was probably irrationally pleased to see someone using a scientific layout for test results (abstract, results, conclusion/discussion).

+ Small wording issue
- Hide

I'd remove "been" from this sentence

Access into the building is exclusive to the Site Director and accompanying Class C/D maintenance personnel who have been consented (Form 3436-A) and briefed to Level 2 security clearance.

I think I'll have to come back to it in a month or so and re-read it again to figure out what I think of it as a whole.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for the critique, I’ll incorporate your suggestions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a pretty interesting SCP! I had a couple of potential minor tonal edits.

Quick Edits
– hide block

SCP-3436 is contained within a 70 m × 85 m, 172 m tall concrete building, which acts as a provisional site. The building — rectangular, beige, and with tinted windows — is designed to blend conspicuously with the surrounding metropolis.

The description of the building seems a little unnecessary, and you should probably call it "Provisional Site-3436" or such since it just contains one SCP. A potential edit might be as follows: "Provisional Site-3436 has been built around SCP-3436, and has been designed to be as inconspicuous as possible."

vermiform massing

A quick Google search reveals that vermiform is more often used in anatomy and biology, so it seems a little out of place here. Replacing this phrase with "worm-like" may be better.

FWD: Evidence Regarding Origins

What is the addendum on SCP-3436-i a forward of? Perhaps just leaving it as "SCP-3436-i" would be better.

The series of statements loosely fits the format of a AWCY Project Proposal. Given that the original
Project Proposal for SCP-3436 would have been presented to an intended patron prior to its
completion, it is likely that the author intended SCP-3436-i as either an additional proposal to
those who read it, or a meta-proposal intended for the religious deity it endorses.

The phrasing here is a little awkward, and AWCY? art installations don't necessarily have patrons, as far as I know. They usually would include at least one usage of the word "cool" as well, though that varies.

Selected Test Logs

Testing logs usually follow a pretty specific format, so you might want to edit the formatting of the current one a bit.

On 07/04/1994, the same day a civilian trespassed upon the provisional site of SCP-3436 and
experienced an unimpeded, pre-enclosure ABE,

You should note that there was some kind of containment breach, since SCP-3436 had apparently already been in containment before that date.

Otherwise, I like this SCP, though the addendum on SCP-3436-i could perhaps stand some more fleshing out and editing. I'm looking forward to seeing more of your writing in the future! +1
